g.data-narration: A binary tree is a data structure in which each node has at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child.
There are several types of binary trees, including full binary trees, empty binary trees, and complete binary trees.
Counting binary trees involves determining the number of possible binary trees that can be created with a given number of nodes.
The array representation of a binary tree stores the nodes in an array, with the left and right child indices stored in the corresponding array elements.
g.text: Node Value | Left Child | Right Child

g.data-narration: The linked list representation of a binary tree stores each node as a linked list node, with the left and right child pointers stored as pointers to the corresponding nodes.
g.text: Node Value | Left Child Pointer | Right Child Pointer

g.data-narration: Each representation has its advantages and disadvantages. The array representation is more memory-efficient, but it is more difficult to insert and delete nodes. The linked list representation is easier to insert and delete nodes, but it is less memory-efficient.
g.text: Trade-offs between memory efficiency and ease of insertion and deletion
